one_line_takeaway: A comprehensive survey that organizes MLLM (vision-language) hallucination into a lifecycle taxonomy of causes -> matching metrics/benchmarks -> cause-linked mitigations; it is a reliability/trustworthiness reference work, not an adversarial-security paper and not a new system.
key_finding: The survey's own analytical observation (from its aggregation of Tables 2 and 3) is that MLLM hallucination performance 'is not always consistent across different benchmarks,' implying different benchmarks measure different dimensions and no single benchmark is decisive. Structurally, the paper's central thesis is that MLLM hallucination differs from pure-LLM hallucination by being fundamentally a cross-modal (text-vs-visual-content) inconsistency, and that mitigations are most coherent when tied back to the specific lifecycle stage (data/model/training/inference) that caused them. From the compiled comparison, stronger/newer models generally hallucinate less: e.g., GPT-4V posts the best CHAIR-on-AMBER (4.3, lower is better) and AMBER Score (92.7) among reported models, versus mPLUG-Owl-7B at 23.1 / 54.1.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (not an empirical system paper); its own contribution is organizational, not a new model or benchmark. The authors build a layered, granular taxonomy of hallucination in multimodal LLMs (MLLMs / LVLMs) along four axes: (1) a taxonomy of CAUSES spanning the full MLLM lifecycle - Data (quantity, quality, statistical bias such as frequent-object and object co-occurrence priors), Model (weak vision encoder / information loss, language/parametric-knowledge prior, weak cross-modal alignment interface), Training (token-level next-token loss lacking sequence-level and RLHF supervision), and Inference (visual-attention deficiency as sequences lengthen, and 'trap' visual tokens); (2) a catalog and categorization of hallucination METRICS and BENCHMARKS (Table 1, ~30 entries); (3) a cause-linked MITIGATION taxonomy organized as Data / Model / Training / Inference so that each remedy maps back to a cause; and (4) two original aggregation tables comparing mainstream MLLMs on generative (Table 2) and discriminative (Table 3) hallucination benchmarks. The authors distinguish their work from a prior concurrent short survey by claiming broader scope, a cause-tracing (rather than architecture-bound) organization, and mitigation strategies explicitly tied to causes. NOTE: POPE, CHAIR, AMBER, LLaVA, InstructBLIP, GPT-4V, LRV-Instruction, etc. are SURVEYED third-party work, not built by these authors.
threat_model: N/A - this is a reliability/trustworthiness survey, not a security paper. Hallucination is framed as an intrinsic cross-modal-consistency failure whose origins lie in data, model, training, and inference, not as the result of an adversary. The paper presents no attacker, no attack surface, and no defense against a malicious party. (The POPE 'adversarial' subset it cites is a hard negative-sampling difficulty setting for benchmarking co-occurrence bias, not an adversarial-attack threat model.) No threat model was invented for this review.
limitations: By the authors' own 'Challenges and Future Directions' section: (1) benchmarks are fragmented with no standardized, theoretically-sound, easy-to-use evaluation - they warn mitigation research risks being 'built on an incorrect foundation,' and note the popular POPE Yes/No protocol does not match how humans actually use MLLMs while free-form generative benchmarks rely on external vision/LLM judges that limit adoption; (2) whether the level of textual detail in training data helps or hurts is explicitly left an OPEN question (too little detail deprives grounding vs. too much exceeds the model's perception limit); (3) current mitigations are empirical pattern-patches (e.g., skipping the '\n' token, penalizing over-trust tokens) with poorly understood mechanisms, motivating interpretability work; (4) they raise ethical concerns (misinformation, bias, privacy) and even a 'hallucination as a feature' reframing. As a survey it inherits the standard limitations: no new experiments, and its comparison tables mix numbers from heterogeneous source papers rather than a controlled re-evaluation. Review-side caveat: the field moves fast (arXiv v2, Apr 2025), so the catalog is a snapshot.

datasets_used: No datasets used by the authors (survey). Underlying data sources of the CATALOGED benchmarks include: MSCOCO (used by CHAIR, POPE, MME, CIEM, M-HalDetect, RAH-Bench, THRONE, BEAF, etc.), Visual Genome (GAVIE, CCEval, LongHalQA, Reefknot), Open-Images (MMHal-Bench, NOPE), LAION (Hal-Eval, mixed), VQAv2 (VQAv2-IDK), ADE20k (ROPE), Objects365 (LongHalQA), TDIUC+AIGC (PhD), and synthetic/web images (AMBER, MOCHa, CorrelationQA).
quantitative_results: All figures below are COMPILED by the survey from the cited benchmarks' own papers (Tables 2/3 captions state this); they are not the survey's own measurements.
Table 2 (generative; CHAIR-on-AMBER lower is better, AMBER Score / HallusionBench-All-Acc / FaithScore higher is better): GPT-4V = CHAIR 4.3, AMBER Score 92.7, HallusionBench All-Acc 65.28.
Table 2: InstructBLIP-7B = CHAIR 10.3, AMBER Score 86.2, HallusionBench 45.26, FaithScore 0.8091 (LLaVA-1k) / 0.9392 (COCO-Cap).
Table 2: mPLUG-Owl-7B = CHAIR 23.1, AMBER Score 54.1, HallusionBench 43.93, FaithScore 0.7167 / 0.8546.
Table 2: LLaVA-1.5-7B = CHAIR 8.6, AMBER Score 82.9, Hal-Eval Gen.Acc 44.6 (in-domain) / 46.4 (out-of-domain).
Table 2: LLaVA-7B = AMBER Score 69.3, Hal-Eval Gen.Acc 23.3 / 26.3; CogVLM-7B = CHAIR 7.9, AMBER Score 86.1.

Benchmark scale examples from Table 1: POPE = 3,000 items (discriminative, Acc/P/R/F1, MSCOCO, drawn from 500 images with random/popular/adversarial negative sampling); CHAIR = 5,000 (generative, MSCOCO, restricted to the 80 COCO object classes); AMBER = 15,202 (disc.+gen., AMBER Score = Avg(1-CHAIR, F1)); MMHal-Bench = 96 image-question pairs (8 question categories x 12 topics, GPT-4 judged); HallusionBench = 1,129 questions over 346 figures / 455 control pairs; MERLIM = 31,373 (survey text elsewhere cites 279K image-question pairs); PhD = 102,564.
CIEM's LLM-based auto-generation pipeline is reported (in the source work) to have ~5% error rate.
baselines_compared: No baselines in the experimental sense - as a survey the authors beat nothing. The MLLMs placed side-by-side in the COMPILED Tables 2/3 include: mPLUG-Owl / mPLUG-Owl2, InstructBLIP (7B/13B), GPT-4V, LLaVA (7B/13B), LLaVA-1.5 (7B/13B), MiniGPT-4 (7B/13B), CogVLM, Qwen-VL-Chat / Qwen-VL2, Open-Flamingo, LRV-Instruction, InternVL2, DeepSeek-VL2, LLaVA-OneVision, and OPERA(LLaVA-1.5), among others - all surveyed third-party models, not baselines authored here.
